12: The elders who had seen the first temple wept when the second temple's foundation was laid — while the younger generation shouted for joy (Ezra 3:12). The mixture of weeping and rejoicing at the restoration point to the ambiguity of the return from exile: it was genuinely wonderful (YHWH's covenant faithfulness demonstrated) but genuinely less than the prophets had promised (the new temple was smaller and less glorious; the Davidic king was absent; the full restoration had not arrived). The prophets Haggai and Zechariah address this exact ambiguity: 'Who has despised the day of small things?' (Zechariah 4:10). The NT's answer is that the greater glory came not through a rebuilt temple but through the incarnation: the Word dwelling among us, the Shekinah glory in human form (John 1:14).
1: Wayyiggaʿ haḥōdeš hašᵉḇîʿî — 'when the seventh month came.' The seventh month (tišrî) is the most sacred month in the Israelite liturgical calendar: it contains the Feast of Trumpets (Numbers 29:1), the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 23:26-32), and the Feast of Tabernacles (Leviticus 23:33-43). The fact that the returning community's first cultic act — rebuilding the altar — occurs precisely in the seventh month is not coincidental. The Solomonic temple dedication also occurred in the seventh month (1 Kings 8:2; 2 Chronicles 5:3). Kᵉʾîš ʾeḥāḏ ʾel-yᵉrûšālaim — 'together as one man to Jerusalem.' The phrase kᵉʾîš ʾeḥāḏ (as one man) is the covenant assembly formula for unified communal action (cf. Judges 20:1; Nehemiah 8:1); the singular collective noun evokes the standing before God as one body. The assembly in the seventh month types the great covenant assembly before YHWH — the community reconstituted as a unified cultic entity before a single stone of the temple has been laid.
2: Wayyāqom yēšûaʿ ben-yôṣāḏāq wᵉʾeḥāyw hakkōhănîm ûzᵉrubbāḇel ben-šᵉʾaltîʾēl ûʾeḥāyw — Jeshua son of Jozadak and his fellow priests, with Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and his kinsmen. The dual leadership (priest + Davidic prince) is explicit from the first cultic act. Jeshua's patronymic traces to Jozadak (1 Chronicles 6:15), the last pre-exilic high priest taken captive — Jeshua's lineage provides Aaronic priestly legitimacy. Zerubbabel's patronymic: son of Shealtiel, who is son of Jehoiachin (Matthew 1:12; Luke 3:27) — the Davidic royal line. The altar is built kaḵātûḇ bᵉtôrat mōšeh ʾîš-hāʾĕlōhîm — 'as written in the law of Moses the man of God.' The authority citation is the Mosaic Torah; the altar's construction follows the Pentateuchal specifications, particularly the prohibition on dressed stone (Ex Ezra 20:25; Deuteronomy 27:5-6) and the specifications for the burnt-offering altar (Ex Ezra 27:1-8).
3: Wayyākînû hammizBēaḥ ʿal-mᵉḵônōtāyw — 'they set the altar on its base/stand.' Mᵉḵônāh (base, platform, stand) is the technical term for the altar's foundation platform (cf. 1 Kings 7:27-29 for the bronze mᵉḵônôt in the Solomonic temple). The altar is set up bᵉʾîmāh ʿălêhem mēʿammê hāʾărāṣôt — 'in fear because of the peoples of the surrounding lands.' The phrase bᵉʾîmāh (in fear/dread) is not the normal word for fear (yirʾāh) but ʾêmāh (dread, terror) — a more acute and pressing fear. The community begins cultic activity under existential threat: the surrounding peoples are hostile (Samaritans, Ammonites, etc.), yet the altar is established before the temple foundation is laid, prioritizing the cult above security. Morning and evening burnt offerings (ʿōlôt baboqer ûḇāʿereḇ) follow the Mosaic tāmîd schedule (Ex Ezra 29:38-42; Numbers 28:3-8).
4: Wayyaʿăśû ʾet-ḥag hasSukkôt kaḵāṯûḇ — 'they observed the Feast of Tabernacles as written.' Sukkôt (Feast of Booths/Tabernacles) is the harvest festival in the seventh month (Leviticus 23:33-43; Deuteronomy 16:13-15), the feast of the completed agricultural year and the community's memorial of wilderness dwelling. The feast of 'as written' (kaḵāṯûḇ) emphasis insists that the post-exilic Sukkot observance follows the Pentateuchal prescription exactly. The mispar (required number) of burnt offerings for each day follows Numbers 29:12-38, which specifies a decreasing sequence: 13 bulls on day 1, declining by one bull each day to 7 on day 7, with a final eighth day. The Feast of Tabernacles is also the feast at which the Solomonic temple was dedicated (1 Kings 8:2), making its celebration the appropriate liturgical context for the altar's rededication.
5: Wᵉʾaḥărê kēn ʿōlat tāmîd — 'and after that, the regular burnt offering.' Having completed the Sukkot sequence, the community establishes the regular (tāmîd) daily offering as the ongoing liturgical baseline. The ʿōlat tāmîd is the twice-daily burnt offering prescribed in Numbers 28:3-8 — the continuous (hence tāmîd = perpetual) offering that defines the cult's existence as uninterrupted service to YHWH. The community also restores offerings for ḥoḏāšîm (new moons) and all môʿăḏê YHWH (appointed feasts of YHWH) — the full liturgical calendar of Lev 23 and Num 28-29 is re-established in sequence. Lᵉkol-mitnaddēḇ nᵉdāḇāh laYHWH — freewill offerings are accepted from all volunteers, restoring the voluntary-gift mechanism alongside the prescribed requirements.
6: Miyyôm ʾeḥāḏ laḥōdeš haššᵉḇîʿî hēḥēllû lᵉhaʿălôt ʿōlôt laYHWH — 'from the first day of the seventh month they began to offer burnt offerings to YHWH.' The first of Tishri is Rosh Hashanah (the Feast of Trumpets, Numbers 29:1) — the New Year's Day of the sacred calendar. Beginning the offering sequence on 1 Tishri anchors the restoration in the covenantal time structure. The parenthetical note ûyᵉsôḏ hêḵal-YHWH lōʾ yussaḏ — 'though the foundation of the temple of YHWH had not yet been laid' — is emphatic: the altar precedes the building. Cult precedes construction; the community worships before they have a sanctuary, just as Israel worshipped at the tabernacle before the temple existed.
7: Wayyittᵉnû kesep laḥōṣᵉḇîm ûlᵉḥārāšîm — 'they gave silver to the stonecutters and carpenters.' The procurement of building materials mirrors the Solomonic temple construction: cedar logs from Lebanon via Sidon and Tyre, transported by sea to Joppa (1 Kings 5:6, 9; 2 Chronicles 2:16). The authorization kᵉrišyôn kôreš melek-pāras ʿălêhem — 'according to the grant (authorization) that Cyrus king of Persia had given them' — invokes the imperial mandate as the legal basis for the construction. Rišyôn is an Aramaic loanword (from rᵉšw = permission/authorization) — a Persian administrative term appearing in Ezra 3:7; its use here reflects the integration of Persian imperial vocabulary into the post-exilic community's legal framework. The provision of food, drink, and oil to the Sidonian and Tyrian workers mirrors Solomon's arrangement with Hiram (1 Kings 5:11).
8: Ûbišnat haššᵉnît lᵉbôʾām ʾel-bêt hāʾĕlōhîm lîrûšālaim — 'in the second year after their arrival at the house of God in Jerusalem, in the second month.' The second year, second month is the precise time-stamp for the foundation-laying. The combination 'second year, second month' recalls that Solomon also began construction 'in the second month' (2 Chronicles 3:2) — the post-Passover month of Iyyar when the rains have ended and outdoor construction can begin. Zerubbabel and Jeshua lead the work with 'all the rest of their fellow Israelites' (šᵉʾār ʾaḥêhem). The age threshold for Levitical service: lᵉḇen ʿeśrîm šānāh wāmāʿᵉlāh — 'from twenty years old and upward.' Numbers 4 sets the Mosaic age for Levitical service at 30; David revised it to 20 (1 Chronicles 23:24-27) for the settled temple context. The post-exilic community follows David's revision — a Davidic rather than Mosaic standard for Levitical age eligibility.
9: Wayyaʿămoḏ yēšûaʿ bānāyw wᵉʾeḥāyw qadmîʾēl ûḇānāyw bᵉnê-yᵉhûdāh yaḥad lᵉnaṣṣēaḥ ʿal-ʿōśê hamᵉlāʾḵāh — Jeshua, his sons and brothers, Kadmiel and his sons (Judahite Levites), and the sons of Henadad with their sons and brothers 'stood together to direct (lᵉnaṣṣēaḥ) the workmen on the house of God.' The verb naṣṣēaḥ (Piel of nāṣaḥ) = to oversee, to be the choirmaster or supervisor — the same term used for the Levitical choir directors in the Psalm superscriptions (lammᵉnaṣṣēaḥ). The building supervisors are designated by the same term used for worship leaders: the construction of the temple is itself a form of worship requiring the same dedicated oversight as the temple music.
10: Wayyassᵉḏû habbōnîm ʾet-hêḵal YHWH — 'when the builders laid the foundation of the temple of YHWH.' The foundation-laying ceremony is a formal liturgical event. The priests take their stations in their vestments (biḵlê hamᵉʿîl) with trumpets (baḥăṣōṣᵉrôt); the Levites, specifically the sons of Asaph, take their stations with cymbals (bimṣiltāyim). The arrangement follows 'the directions of David king of Israel' (ʿal yᵉḏê dāwîḏ melek yiśrāʾēl) — the Davidic liturgical order. The trumpets are the Aaronic priestly instruments (Numbers 10:1-10); the cymbals are associated with the Levitical singing guilds (1 Chronicles 15:19). The combination of priestly trumpet and Levitical cymbal for the foundation ceremony follows the pattern of the Solomonic dedication (2 Chronicles 5:12-13).
11: Wayyaʿănû bᵉhallēl ûḇᵉhôḏōt laYHWH kî ṭôḇ kî lᵉʿôlām ḥasdô ʿal-yiśrāʾēl — 'they sang antiphonally, praising and giving thanks to YHWH, for he is good, for his steadfast love toward Israel endures forever.' The antiphonal praise formula kî ṭôḇ kî lᵉʿôlām ḥasdô is the standard temple praise refrain (Psalms 100:5; Ezra 106:1; Ezra 107:1; Ezra 118:1-4, 29; Ezra 136:1-26; 2 Chronicles 5:13; Ezra 7:3). The formula's repetition in this precise context is the Chronicler's signature liturgical anchor: this is the refrain sung at the Solomonic dedication (2 Chronicles 5:13) and again at Hezekiah's Passover (2 Chronicles 30:21) — now sung again at the second temple's foundation. The continuous refrain spanning three major cultic events expresses the theological claim that YHWH's ḥesed is the continuity linking the original covenant to its post-exilic renewal. Wayyārîʿû kol-hāʿām tᵉrûʿāh gᵉḏôlāh — all the people shouted with a great shout (tᵉrûʿāh): the covenant shout of celebration (cf. Numbers 23:21; 1 Samuel 4:5; the Feast of Trumpets in Numbers 29:1 is the day of tᵉrûʿāh).
13: Wᵉʾên hāʿām maḵkîrîm qôl tᵉrûʿat haśśimḥāh lᵉqôl bᵉḵî hāʿām — 'no one could distinguish the sound of the joyful shouting from the sound of the people's weeping, for the people raised such a great shout that the sound was heard far away.' The impossibility of distinguishing joy from grief is the final note of the chapter — a theologically resonant ambiguity. The sound that carries 'far away' (mirrāḥôq) is the mingled cry of restored hope and irretrievable loss. The weeping for the first temple represents authentic grief over what the exile destroyed; the joy over the new foundation represents authentic hope in YHWH's faithfulness. The Chronicler does not resolve the tension — the eschatological community holds both responses simultaneously, as the prophets anticipated: 'those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy' (Psalms 126:5); Zechariah will later rebuke those who treat the new temple as less worthy (Zechariah 4:10 — 'who has despised the day of small things?').
